speaker: interviewer
text: And we're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, this is tape number six--

speaker: interviewee
text: Six.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Of our interview with survivor Jacob Sandbrand. Today's date is January 22, 1997. We're in Rancho Bernardo/San Diego, California. Jack, we were talking about your coming to the United States. And you, you met the family, the Weiss family, in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Brooklyn. And they got you in touch with your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: They got me in touch with my Aunt Fanny Schafer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she came over that Sunday, to pick me up, and brought me over to my other aunt, which was the oldest sister of my mother's, the one that came to the United States in 1897. Her name was Sarah Mandel. And her husband was a rabbi, David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Brooklyn. He had a little shtiebel shul on Coney Island Avenue. And I, by coincidence, my third aunt was there. The one that I remembered her name, but she didn't live in the Bronx anymore. She lived in California-- in Florida at that time. And she came in for a ceremony. Because her younger son perished in the American army in Italy. She came in for a kaddish or something, for a Yizkor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I met the whole family at the same time. And at that get-together, I met my cousin Mike Schafer, that was a furrier. He was in business in Manhattan on-- at that time on 29th Street and Seventh Avenue. And he also asked me, do I have any trade, what I do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, on my listing from the German-- from the American consulate in Germany, he asked me what I did, and I said a waiter. I didn't know what to say, so I said a waiter.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, well, would you like to learn how to become a furrier? I had no idea what a furrier was. [LAUGHS] I says, OK, give me a job. So I became a furrier. Is that a career that you kept?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that is a career I kept for 25 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked for my cousin, Mike, for 2 and 1/2 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the-- on 29th Street and Seventh Avenue in Manhattan.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I got sick. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I picked up a case of tuberculosis. They caught it just in time. I had-- didn't have a cavity, I had a shadow on my lungs. And the union I belonged to, the Furriers' Union in New York, made arrangements for me to go to this place in Browns Mills, New Jersey. It was a tuberculosis place. And I spent there six months, mostly rest and food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after the sanatorium, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I came home. I stayed with my aunt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my aunt and the other aunt, who lived in Florida, decided that it was wintertime, that I'd be better off come down for a few months to visit her in Florida, in Miami. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took a train and wound up in Miami [LAUGHS] that winter. I think it was October or November, I'm not sure, of 1948. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And daily I went to the beach [LAUGHS] and had a good time. And that's where I met my wife, on the beach. [LAUGHS] How, how long was it after you met her that you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you date her, did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did her for a few months in Florida. And then she and her parents went back to Toronto, Canada. And we corresponded back and forth, from New York. When I came back to New York, we corresponded. And the following June, I drove out to Toronto. And we got married Toronto, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. And then you went back to New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I had a job in New York. My aunt wrote a letter that if I come back quickly, my job will be waiting for me. And Bernice came down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you worked as a furrier in New York for--

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York City.

speaker: interviewer
text: -- For many years. Did you have a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no one there. Well, I had my cousins, my aunts and uncles.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, did you have your own family? Did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well at that time-- yeah, well, a year later, my daughter-- our daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mirna Lise Sandbrand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was born in 1949?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was born in 1950. We were-- we were married in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry, I skipped a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's okay. I tried to start a little earlier, but it didn't work. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you have any other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have an adopted son, Michael, which he was adopted much, much later. He was our foster son. We took him in from the Jewish Childcare Association. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after Michael was taken home by his parents, we took in another child, Konnie Benitos, who came from a mixed family. Her father was Greek Orthodox and her mother was Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we took her into our home. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she went back to her parents, after two or three years. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] From Konnie, we didn't hear from a long time. From Michael, we heard every few months, that he was OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he was 18, he came back to us. And he said he wants to be with us. The Jewish Childcare Association had nothing to do with it anymore. And he got married. And we asked him if he wanted to be adopted, and he said, yes. [LAUGHS] And we adopted him at the age of 26. Konnie, after she went back to her parents, her father divorced her mother. And he remarried, and he moved to Colorado. We had no idea where she was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After 32 years, she looked for us. She was already married, with three kids. And she started looking in every state of the union. She had no idea where we were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, her husband said to her-- her husband is a commander in the Navy, a nuclear submarine. He said to her, now where would Jewish people move from New York when they're retired? Florida. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we weren't in Florida, we were in California. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But he called Florida anyway. They called different areas. And sure enough, our son, adopted son, Michael, lived in Florida at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: They found his name, Michael Sandbrand, with his wife. And she got the number. And she wasn't sure if she wanted to call up. She says, what happens if they don't want us? She called up, and she spoke to Michael's wife, Miriam. Do you know a Jack and Bernice Sandbrand? And she says, yes, those are our in-laws. She explained who we were. And Michael knew about Konnie. We told him about Konnie, that she was-- she came to us afterwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, we wanted to adopt Konnie too. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the parents said, no, they wouldn't give her up. And besides that, what I think is her father was afraid that we were going to bring her up Jewish. He was a Greek Orthodox. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well anyway, Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: Konnie gave Michael the number. And when we got to Florida, we called her. And two days later, we were in Konnie's house. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a question, Jack. You know, so much of what happens in your life has been involved with Judaism in a sense, because of your identification. You're, you're Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Has, has Judaism played an important role in your life here in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very, very mixed up. It was a yes and no time in my life. I knew I wanted to be a Jew, because I didn't want to give Hitler the satisfaction that I'm not a Jew. Because if I wasn't a Jew, he'd win. He tried to exterminate us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yet, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in my mind, I kept saying to myself, where was God to permit the atrocities that happened? Why? For what reason? Were we bad Jews? Now, maybe I was a bad Jew, as a youngster. But my parents were very religious. My grandfather went to shul most of his life. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, my grandfather was lucky. He died before the war started. But my grandma, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother, my brother. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm still debating that issue. I met just this last weekend this rabbi from the Holly-- the Holy Blossom Temple, from Toronto. What was his name? Rabbi-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I asked about that question. And he said to me, if you don't believe in God, no matter what I tell you will not help you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He says there's no answer to it, why. I've heard many answers. God didn't kill the people. People killed people. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm still looking for an answer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, if you had to try to condense what you've learned out of life in a couple of easy sentences, what would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: You live your life as best as you can. And you hope it doesn't happen again. Because the first 10 years of my life in the United States, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't know what America was all about. I had no idea how the workings were of the democracy, you know, and all that stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very frightened. Could it happen again here, in the United States? Especially when you hear all these people coming out with, with statements about Jews and Judaism. Like Buchanan lately, you know who I'm talking about. Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Or like Buchanan saying-- making a statement during the war, the Gulf War, that only the people-- how did he put it-- of the, the 'amen people' want, want us to fight for them, in, in Iraq. The 'amen people.'

speaker: interviewer
text: Jack, I want to thank you very much for sharing your, your life experience with us and for telling us--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're quite welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: --What you went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: For many years, I couldn't talk about it. My children never knew what was going on, until later on. But they did ask me. My daughter wanted to know, how come I don't have aunts or uncles from your-- from my father's side, or grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle-- my father's brother, which I met here in the United States-- said to me, why don't you change your name to Zeichner? That's your name. I couldn't do it. I lived all my life with Sandbrand. I would be-- my, my-- I would be defeating my mother, you know, I would be-- [SOBBING] I understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jack, thank you very much for sharing.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are quite welcome. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jack, would you introduce us to the lovely lady to your left? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife. We are married 48 years. Her name was Bernice Richmond, and her name is Bernice Sandbrand. And we hope we can make it up to 50.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bernice, where, where are you from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toronto, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during the war, where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Toronto, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, in living your life with Jack, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what impact has Jack's war experience had on your life together, if any?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. It's become part of my life. It's like I've experienced the things that sometimes I forget. And I think it's me and not him. All of us, my daughter, too, are very-- and his zest for life. He's such a positive person. He's, he's such a happy-go-lucky. And he's, he's very, very sensitive. It's nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, many people did not survive the war. Do you think it was something about the way Jack thinks about living that helped him make it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that the fact that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in spite of all that was thrown at him, he felt that he had to survive and fight and be tough. He was tough. He was always tough, even though-- sensitive, but tough. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Could I say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Jack, go ahead, sure. Jump in any time.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think my upbringing, so to speak, in the Russian army, the training, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the running, the, the overall-- I call it upbringing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Motivation.

speaker: interviewee
text: --making you a man from a child, from a young boy, had something to do with me being the way I am.

speaker: interviewee
text: Motivations.

speaker: interviewee
text: It made you push to be-- not to give up, to survive, to work, not to beg. To be somebody. To struggle. And I think that's what had something to do with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you learned-- I'm sorry, Bernice?

speaker: interviewee
text: His whole life. No, he's been like this his whole life. He told you how he worked, very, very hard, all the time. And to make sure that we had. And make sure that-- that the sweetest thing with him is when we had our child. It was life again. That was very special to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you close to your children now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Bernice, I want to thank you very much. Is there anything you want to say to Jack on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mind you, we also had our ups and downs with our children, like every family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there is no perfect [LAUGHS] thing. You worry what they're going to do, and you worry how they're going to live. And you worry about it. And then it turns out whatever you give them, it comes back.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: What you put into them.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you don't put it hard on them, you don't put them down, and you are patient, they'll come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you love them.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Well, love is the most important thing in life.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's got a lot of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bernice, I want to thank you very much for coming on, for sharing with us. And Jack, again, I want to thank you very much, for sharing your life with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we hope for the best.

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the future. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got him, I got my future.

speaker: interviewer
text: There you go. All right, now Jack, what I want--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my grandfather, my father's father. His name was Nathan Zeichner. He died before World War I. This is my father, Ephraim Zalman Zeichner. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He died on May 26, 1925. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was hit with a bomb shrapnel. The way I got the picture is when I came to the country, that picture was in possession of my Uncle, Joe Zeichner. And he passed it on to me when he passed away. That's me, Jack Sandbrand. I was in the Russian army. I believe this picture was taken in 1945, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] right before the war was over-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe two months before the war was over-- in the outskirts, not too far from Berlin. I was a sergeant, a [RUSSIAN] in Russian. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I was still alive, [LAUGHS] thank God. That's about it. This is my uncle, Joe Zeichner, brother of my father's. He came to the States way before, many years before World War I. And I met him first when I came here in 1945. And he's the one who gave me my father's and my grandfather's picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived in the Bronx, on Fairmont Place, on a hill above Tremont Avenue. He worked all his life for the United States Post Office. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he died at the age of 83, I believe. That's it. This is Mr. and Mrs. Weiss. They lived in Fort Hamilton Parkway. And they are the aunt and uncle of the three young women that I helped, when we liberated them from a concentration camp. This camp had only Hungarian women. This was nearly the end of the war. And they were very sick. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I kept in correspondence with them, after I came to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I did not keep in correspondence with the girls, because they were still in Germany. And about five years later, they went to Israel. And I didn't keep contact with them. When I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] helped liberate the three young women from the concentration camp, one of them gave me a address of her brother in Tel Aviv. I wrote him a letter. And he sent me back this telegram, which I received just before the war was over. It was sent to-- through Moscow, through my army post.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the question on the telegram is, thank you for helping my sisters. What happened to our parents? Which I had no idea what happened to his parents. And that's all I can tell you about the telegram.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same time, he notified his uncle, Mr. And Mrs. Weiss, in Brooklyn. And they sent me a letter, thanking me for helping those three young women.
